series.title: [PATCH 0/5] intel_iommu: Enable PRQ support for passthrough device

review.summary: The series is close, but patch 3 introduces a functional hole in the FAULTQ read path: it hard-codes a 100-entry buffer and treats an oversized group as a one-off log condition instead of a recoverable protocol case. That can leave PRQ delivery permanently stalled for a PASID. I also see a likely enablement mismatch where host FAULTQ setup is keyed off SVM+PRE rather than guest PRQ enable state, which risks delivering recoverable faults before the guest queue is ready. Patches 4 and 5 are not present in the supplied diff, so their detailed line-level review is necessarily limited.

stages.A: Patch 1/5 looks fine. The new IOMMU_FAULT_QUEUE_ALLOC helper is straightforward and follows existing backend patterns.
stages.B: Patch 2/5 looks mechanically correct. Auto-setting IOMMU_HWPT_FAULT_ID_VALID from a nonzero fault_id is reasonable and the call-site updates are consistent.
stages.C: Patch 3/5 has at least one substantive correctness issue in the FAULTQ read path, and a likely PRQ enablement race/mismatch.
stages.D: Patch 4/5 was not included in the supplied diff. Based on the cover letter, response routing and validation need close review, especially group-id/faultq matching and failure handling.
stages.E: Patch 5/5 was not included in the supplied diff. Based on the cover letter, bottom-half teardown is the right area to scrutinize for fd-handler lifetime and close-vs-callback races.

findings.title: Oversized fault groups are not recoverable and can wedge PRQ delivery
findings.description: The FAULTQ reader uses a fixed 100-entry stack buffer and explicitly assumes that is merely "large enough in regular test". When the kernel reports an oversized fault group by returning 0 bytes, the handler only logs once and returns. There is no retry with a larger buffer, no drain path, and no disabling of the fd handler. In practice this leaves the same unreadable group pending, so fault delivery for that PASID can stall indefinitely and may also keep retriggering the main-loop callback.
patch_context: +#define FAULTQ_BUF_SIZE 100 /* Large enough in regular test */
+        error_report_once("FAULTQ(id %u): fault group too big", id);
findings.suggestion: Do not hard-code the queue read depth. Either query/track the required group size and retry with a dynamically allocated buffer, or treat a 0-byte read as a hard protocol condition that disables the handler and tears down/rebuilds the FAULTQ cleanly. At minimum, avoid returning to the main loop with the same unreadable group still pending.
findings.confidence_reason: The bug is visible directly in the added code and matches the handler's own comment and error path: a fixed-size buffer plus a non-recovering 0-byte read path leaves no way to progress on oversized groups.

findings.title: FAULTQ allocation is gated by SVM+PRE, not by guest PRQ enable state
findings.description: The commit message says FAULTQ should be allocated when the guest enables PRQ in the virtual IOMMU, but the actual gate is only `s->svm` plus a context-entry PRE check. That means host recoverable faults may start being routed toward the guest before the guest PRQ is configured and ready to consume them. If `pri_request_page` assumes a live guest PRQ, this can drop requests or create hard-to-debug ordering issues during bring-up.
findings.suggestion: Gate FAULTQ creation on the actual guest PRQ enable state, not just SVM/PRE capability. If that state is not yet available at attach time, defer FAULTQ setup until PRQ enable or explicitly suppress host-to-guest fault injection until the virtual PRQ has been programmed.
findings.confidence_reason: The mismatch between the commit message and the implemented gate is explicit in the diff. The exact runtime impact depends on how `pri_request_page` behaves when PRQ is not yet enabled, so severity is moderate rather than high.

cover_letter:
Subject: [PATCH 0/5] intel_iommu: Enable PRQ support for passthrough device
From: Zhenzhong Duan <zhenzhong.duan@intel.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Mar 2026 22:52:22 -0400
Message-Id: <20260327025228.474257-1-zhenzhong.duan@intel.com>
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit

Hi,

When svm=on,pasid=N,x-flst=on is configured for virtual vtd, we enable
guest's support for vSVM. In this case, the host VTD may generate
recoverable first stage page fault event, QEMU read the event and inject
it to guest.

After guest handles the event, it sends a page group response, QEMU gets
the response and pass it to host VTD.

This series adds QEMU support for receiving such host events through the
FAULTQ interface and propagating them to the guest, then catching guest's
responses and propagating them to host.

This patchset depends on pasid support:
https://lore.kernel.org/qemu-devel/20260326091130.321483-1-zhenzhong.duan@intel.com/#t

GIT branch: https://github.com/yiliu1765/qemu/tree/zhenzhong/iommufd_prq

Tests:
Tested with DSA wq attached to an user APP which triggers IO page fault on
process page table durging DMA.

Thanks
Zhenzhong


Zhenzhong Duan (5):
  backends/iommufd: Introduce iommufd_backend_alloc_faultq
  backends/iommufd: Extend iommufd_backend_alloc_hwpt() with fault_id
  intel_iommu_accel: Add PRQ injection for passthrough device
  intel_iommu_accel: Accept PRQ response for passthrough device
  intel_iommu_accel: teardown FAULTQ resources in bottom half
